title: Improving language models by retrieving from trillions of tokens
hook: RETRO lets language models look up real documents as they generate new text.
promise: You will understand how retrieval augments a language model and reduces the need for massive parameter counts.
prerequisite_note: You just need to know that language models learn from lots of text and use parameters to store knowledge.

glossary.plain: A language model that searches an external database for relevant information while generating text.
A method to quickly find text passages in a huge database that are most similar to the current context.
Splitting text into smaller, manageable pieces that can be indexed and retrieved.
Model weights that are not updated during certain training phases or when using external data.
The total amount of storage or parameters a model requires to operate.
A measure of how well a language model predicts text, with lower values indicating better performance.

cards.title: Let language models look things up
body: See how language models can use retrieval to generate better text with fewer parameters.

cards.title: Why not just ask the database?
body: Imagine you have to write an essay, but you can only use what you remember. What if you could also look up facts instantly from a library?
RETRO gives language models this power: instead of memorizing everything, they retrieve relevant information from a huge database as they write.

cards.title: How retrieval shapes generation
body: RETRO splits the input into chunks, searches a giant database for similar passages, and feeds those into the model as it generates text.
This means the model does not need to store every fact in its parameters. It leans on retrieval to fill in details on demand.
cards.caption: RETRO's flow: chunk input, retrieve context, then generate richer output.

cards.title: Would you rather memorize or look up?
body: Toggle to see how classic language models and RETRO handle rare facts.
cards.offNote: Model must store every detail in its parameters, needing huge models.
cards.onNote: Model finds rare facts in external data, so fewer parameters are needed.

cards.title: The central trick: swap memory for search
body: Classic language models get larger as they try to memorize more information from their training data, making them expensive to train and run.
RETRO keeps the model smaller by teaching it to search a database for relevant text, essentially trading stored parameters for fast retrieval.

cards.title: How much should the model memorize?
body: Slide to see how the balance between retrieval and parameters affects model size and performance.
cards.low: The model stores everything in its parameters, so it is huge and costly.
cards.mid: The model memorizes common facts but retrieves rare details from the database.
cards.high: The model stores little and relies heavily on fast retrieval for almost all knowledge.

cards.title: What does RETRO need?
body: Pick the ingredients that make RETRO work.

choices.label: Large text database
choices.note: Holds trillions of tokens for the model to search.

choices.label: Chunked input text
choices.note: Allows the model to break queries into searchable pieces.

choices.label: Nearest neighbor search
choices.note: Selects the most relevant text chunks for each input.

choices.label: Smaller model parameters
choices.note: Needs fewer stored weights due to retrieval.

cards.title: RETRO's impact on language models
body: RETRO matches or beats the performance of models with ten times more parameters by using retrieval.
It achieves lower perplexity, better factual accuracy, and needs much less memory than classic models of similar skill.

bars.label: Parameter count reduction
bars.note: Matches performance with far fewer parameters

bars.label: Factual accuracy
bars.note: Improves on rare or obscure facts

bars.label: Memory footprint
bars.note: Requires less GPU memory to run

cards.caption: RETRO delivers strong results with a smaller, more efficient language model.

cards.title: Why RETRO changed the game
body: RETRO proved that language models do not have to memorize everything. They can be smaller, faster, and just as smart by looking things up.
This idea inspired a wave of retrieval-augmented models, making powerful language AI more accessible and efficient.

recap.label: Retrieve
recap.note: Find similar passages from a giant database

recap.label: Augment
recap.note: Feed retrieved context into the model

recap.label: Generate
recap.note: Produce more accurate and informed text

cards.title: Improving language models by retrieving from trillions of tokens
body: Read the full paper for technical details and experiments behind RETRO's breakthrough.
